backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text: The anterior (pectoral) group lies along the lateral thoracic artery. These nodes receive lymph primarily from the breast, anterior chest wall, and upper abdomen. Clinical pearl: This is the first group palpated during breast examination — enlargement is an early sign of breast pathology. Memory aid: "Pectoral = Pecs = chest and breast drainage."

The lateral group lies medial to the axillary vein and receives lymph from the upper limb. Nearly all lymph from the arm, forearm, and hand passes through these nodes. Clinical pearl: An infected wound on the hand can cause tender enlargement of this group. Memory aid: "Lateral = Limb."

The central group is embedded in axillary fat and receives lymph from the anterior, lateral, and posterior groups — acting as a relay station. These are the most easily palpable nodes. Clinical pearl: These are the nodes you feel when pressing high into the axilla against the chest wall. Memory aid: "Central = Collector."

The apical group lies at the apex of the axilla and is the final relay before lymph enters the subclavian lymph trunk. All other axillary groups ultimately drain here. Clinical pearl: Apical node involvement indicates advanced disease — N3 in breast cancer TNM staging. Memory aid: "Apical = final gatekeeper."

The posterior (subscapular) group lies along the subscapular artery. These nodes drain the back of the trunk, posterior shoulder, and lower neck. Clinical pearl: Melanoma on the upper back may metastasise to this group. Memory aid: "Subscapular = Scapula = back."
